column1: A function is called a kernel
column4: (1) It is declared with the __global__ keyword,(2) It is launched from the CPU using the triple-chevron syntax <<<...>>>,(3) It is intended to run in parallel on many threads on the GPU.

column1: When a kernel is launched with <<<N, M>>>
column4: the GPU creates N blocks with M threads each, for a total of N×M threads. All these threads execute the kernel function in parallel. Each thread can identify itself using blockIdx.x and threadIdx.x to process different data.

column1: What is a kernel in CUDA
column4: A kernel is a function declared with the __global__ qualifier that is executed on the GPU. It is launched from the host (CPU) using the execution configuration syntax <<<gridDim, blockDim>>>. All threads created by the launch execute the same kernel code in parallel.

column1: What happens when you launch a kernel with <<<N, M>>>
column4: The launch creates a grid consisting of N blocks, where each block contains M threads. This results in a total of N × M threads. Each thread executes the kernel function concurrently on the GPU.

column1: What is the difference between a Block and a Thread
column4: A Thread is the smallest unit of execution.A Block is a group of threads that can cooperate with each other (share shared memory and synchronize using __syncthreads()). Blocks are independent of each other and cannot directly synchronize.

column1: How does a thread identify which data it should process?
column4: Each thread calculates a unique index, typically using:  int globalId = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x;  blockIdx.x gives the block index, threadIdx.x gives the thread index within the block, and blockDim.x is the number of threads per block.

column1: What is a Grid in CUDA?A Grid is the collection of all blocks launched in a single kernel invocation. It is the highest level in the CUDA hierarchy: Grid → Blocks → Threads.
Can blocks synchronize with each other?
column4: No. Blocks are independent and cannot synchronize with each other. Only threads within the same block can synchronize using __syncthreads().

column1: What is the maximum number of threads per block?
column4: The typical hardware limit is 1024 threads per block. The exact limit can be queried using cudaDeviceProp.

column1: What is the difference between __global__, __device__, and __host__?
column4: __global__: Kernel function. Launched from host, runs on device.  __device__: Function that runs on the GPU, can only be called from other device functions or kernels.  __host__: Regular CPU function (default if no qualifier is used).

column1: Is kernel launch synchronous or asynchronous?
column4: Kernel launches are asynchronous by default. The CPU continues execution immediately after the launch. We use cudaDeviceSynchronize() to wait for the GPU to finish.

column1: Why do we use cudaMalloc and cudaMemcpy?
column4: cudaMalloc allocates memory on the GPU device. cudaMemcpy transfers data between host (CPU) memory and device (GPU) memory. The GPU cannot directly access host memory in this simple model.
